sentences.text: So, there we were.
We were living in Budapest.
And as I said, I really did not feel that there was a war that was even going on.
From time to time, however, I was being indoctrinated.
As-- as I said, I didn't have to wear a star.
So who are you, I'm a Hungarian.
Who are our allies?
The Germans and the Austrians.
Who are the enemies?
The French and the English.
So things like this, I was indoctrinated with things like this.
By whom?
By my parents.
Also there were rumors of booby traps.
Don't pick up-- if you go on a railway station, or you go by the bus, or where have you, don't pick up a doll, don't pick up a pen, don't pick anything on the street.
It's a-- they're booby trapped.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so I really can't say anything anymore.
I remember the-- we had a large Seder in 1944 for Passover.
And then, we were going some place.
We were going away.
We were going some place.
The whole family, we were gonna leave Europe.
And-- so one day we had to go to a gathering place.
And I don't know where it is, I think it was outside of Budapest.
And there were over a thousand people there.
Some friends, but mainly people that I didn't know.
There were some friends of my parents, but I didn't know any of the people there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we took not too many belongings, but a suitcase per person, what have you.
Oh, I'd like to just go back, yes.
That we did know there was a war going on.
I mean every night, you slept in your underwear.
And every night, like clockwork, after a while in beginning in early of '44, the English Air Force would come over and bomb the area, or bomb-- or at least come over.
And so we'd go down to the basement.
And we had little knapsacks.
And we'd have these knapsacks.
We'd put them on our backs.
So it was not made out of leather, but out of cloth knapsacks.
[INAUDIBLE] And we'd go downstairs with underwear and our pajamas into the basement, because there was no real shelters.
I mean, we went to the basement until the bombing raids were over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so I misspoke before about-- the more I talk about it, the more I remember-- about not being aware there was a war.
Of course there was a war.
We already-- the war was only on at night because the nights when we'd have the bombing raids.
Was it fun?
There was a-- it was [INAUDIBLE]-- it was as a way.
Everybody was doing it.
So it was not that it was something special, something different.
I was very afraid in Belgrade the first day of the bombing.
I was very, very afraid.
Because actually, the bombing here, I don't recall any  bombings.
I don't recall seeing any bombed out buildings or what have you, just probably just the planes flying over going wherever they were going to bomb.
But I don't believe, at least I don't remember, Budapest having been bombed during the time that we were there.
So anyway, we went to this assembling station.
And-- it was in the summertime.
The cherries were ripe for picking.
And we were all put into these cattle cars.
And we started going east.
Excuse me, who was there?
Who in your family was there?
My sister, my mother, my father, and I.

My mother's mother, my father's mother, who was supposed to be with us as well, because of a little bombing that happened and that the railway station was bombed in Subotica, they could not get out to come and join us.
So their fate was entirely different than ours.
We left Budapest.
It was quite crammed.
We were in cattle cars.
And we came to the Austrian border.
And for whatever reason, I don't know, two or three days they were haggling about letting us-- well, we were with this very, very special transport.
We were not supposed to go to concentration camp, we were leaving.
We were gonna go to Uganda.
We were going to a neutral country.
We were going to go away.

We were going to be pawns, or a token of good faith, in a negotiation that were going on between Saly Mayer of the Joint Distribution Agency and a man called Kasztner and man called Joel Brand to save-- there was gonna be a devil with the bargain.
The Germans desperately needed winterized trucks for the Eastern Front coming for the winter of '44.
And they were going to trade away and give up all the Jews for the trucks.
All the Hungarian Jews.
So there were almost, I mean, there were-- I think close to a million Jews still around in Hungary mainly at liberty.
And they wanted a trade.
Yes.
No.
That was the deal.
I mean the deal was we were a part of the goodwill gesture that, as a matter of fact negotiations had continued.
We are going and when we arrive, money or trucks, or whatever was going to go into the hands of the-- of the Gestapo, and the SS, and the-- and the German army.
Well, knew for sure?
No.
They knew that they were killing Jews left and right, but the-- the scope of the extermination of Jews was not known.
I-- definitely not by my parents.
I can guarantee 100% they didn't even know about the gassings, and I'll tell you later on why.
You know, [INTERPOSING VOICES] Goethe once said, Man sieht was man weiss.
If you know something, you see it.
If-- if you-- if it is-- if it is humanly non-believable, even if you see it, you're not gonna believe it.
So nobody wants to die.
Nobody wants to believe the worst possible thing from another human being.
I don't think anybody knew.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Our group was a very interesting makeup of group.
It was Zionists, Hungarian Zionists.
It was some family of the gentlemen who were negotiating, Kasztner and Brand.
It was the Satmar Rabbi Yoel Teitelbaum who hated the Zionists, not hated, he disliked the Zionists.
He didn't believe in Zion because until the Messiah were to come, there's not gonna be a Zion.
He and his entourage were saved by the Hungarian Zionists in this group.
And I don't know perhaps, 50 or 100 Polish orphan children who somehow managed to get to Budapest.
So as I said, the train came.
And the train arrived.
And then there was some negotiating problems with the Austrians.
And we were on the Austrian Hungarian border for several days.
The train was just stopped.
It was very hot summertime.
Were you allowed to get out of the train?
We were allowed to get out of the train.
We had food.
Not great food, we had food.

And the reason I mention that is was summer, one of the young men, he must've been 13 or 14, I remember he took a handkerchief with four corners into it and climbed the tree, and he was picking cherries.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And just eating cherries, picking cherries and eating cherries.
We were there for several days.
And my father delivered a little girl on the train, and he used shoelaces to tie the umbilical cord.
My father was a pediatrician.
He was not an obstetrician.
And a footnote, that little girl is a judge today.
And she lives in Australia.
So whatever happened, the Hungarians and the Austrians got together, and they agreed for to let the train go.
We went.
The next thing I remember, we arrived in a town called Linz, L-I-N-Z, in Austria.
And there, you first realize that we're not going where we're supposed to be going.
At least for 24 hours, you're locked up in some place.
And showers and delousing.
And they started shaving people's hair-- heads and what have you.
Being so young, I was with the women.
Well, they got us off the trains.
A guy got us to this place.
And then they started, you know, the physical examination, the delousing, the shaving of the hair.
Well the men and women, well yes.
The men and women were separated.
The women were certain area with all the female children and the male children up to a certain age, what have you.
I-- I was young enough.
I was [INAUDIBLE] with the women.
I don't know how long we were there, a day, two days.
I really don't know how long.
Back to the train.
And then for some reason, we decided not to go to freedom, they send us to a place called Bergen-Belsen in Hannover, Germany.
So I don't know how many days that-- that leg of the trip took.
But the train--
Was I frightened then?
No.
I was-- I was-- I have to tell you.
The only time that I was frightened, I was frightened twice.
Once the day of the bombing.
And once when-- and I'll tell you it's something else that happened at [INTERPOSING VOICES] in a little while about what happened.
But I was only frightened twice.
The first bombing in Belgrade.
In Belgrade.
In Belgrade.
We arrived at the depot, train depot.
And it was-- being a kid, you know everything is much bigger, much further, much more.
I really don't know how far, but it was quite a distance away from where we were.
We picked up our equipment, luggage, and what have you, and we're just marching and marching.
And primarily on-- as we were coming in, it was primarily on to our left.
Typical barracks, barbed wire, the watch tower.
I just-- I don't know how many, but finally we got to a place and that was it.
There was nobody there.
We came in.
Again, the barracks, I think were divided alphabetically.
I think alphabetically.
And men, one place.
Women with young children in another barrack.
In the men's barracks, there were double decker beds.
In the women's barracks, there were triple decker beds.
I don't know how many hundreds of people in the hut or barrack.
There was one toilet that was functioning, because at night, we were not allowed out.
We were not allowed.
There was one large washroom with the running cold water and taps for women, one for men.
And also a latrine there.
There was also a smaller area where between the men's and women's barracks where you could wash, but, you know, didn't have latrines, and didn't have--
It was-- it was-- you could wash it outdoor, but it was as if a fence had been built with reeds, posted reeds.
Quite a bit further away were the latrines where you sat to move your bowels.
It was just like open, like slip trenches.
You know, like slip trenches.
It was covered.
And near there to the end were the watch towers.
To our right were Russians.
And to the left were Dutch.
Now, we were very, very privileged.
We were the most privileged people ever, I think, in a concentration camp.
Nobody had to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Even when things broke down, we didn't have to be repair it.
Nobody in the camp.
Nobody in our group had to work, except for my father.
He was a physician.
We had something that was so unusual.
We had a hospital barrack.
Our own-- our own section, our own section.
We had one barrack that was a hospital.
As a matter of fact, I saw my-- my second birth there.
About 1,300, I believe.
[INAUDIBLE] At least Hungarians, were together--
Hungarian Jews.
Well, everybody was supposed to have been Hungarian.
We were together and life was rather boring.
I mean, you were hungry because there was very little food.
But every morning, every morning at 5 o'clock, rain or shine, it was appel you have to get up.
And all counting the people and counting the people and counting--
Outside.
Everybody have to get on line.
Counting for hours in the rain or cold.
They didn't care.
You know, and if there was one mistake, start all over again.
You have to line up in rows of five, five deep.
And the father of the little girl that was born on the train, he just happened to be a B.
His-- his last name was B-O-D-O-R, so we were in the B barrack with the B's, so he was the man in charge of counting.
Because they'd never let a woman count women's, you know, they wouldn't let-- let a woman count numbers or do anything.
Counting.
No, no, no, he wasn't in charge.
He was in charge of counting.

I mean, that was his-- that he had-- that-- you know, he had to make sure that this barrack, [BACKGROUND NOISE] there were x number of people that were counted in the evening and that's, you know, tomorrow either you show me a dead body or you have to have the same number of people.
Mainly our life was very boring, very hungry, very boring.
I learned a few words of Polish from these orphans.
We sang songs of the Halutzim.
We learned how to dance the horah.
Children mainly played.
I played soccer.
No, no, no.
We have-- you-- you had your own enclosure.
We had our own enclosure.
And really, we could walk as-- you know, do what we wanted to do.
Oh, sure there was a fence.
Of course, of course.
Electrified fence.
To our right, as I said were the Russians and to our left were Dutch.
I mean our own area, you know, you could go as you want.
The only time you-- you couldn't go out after sundown.
And of course, you had to be there for appel.
Played soccer, and I kicked the ball over.
And the guard kindly handed it over at the end of a bayonet.
Deflated the ball.
And that was the second time that I was afraid, that everybody was going to hate me because I ruined the thing for them.
Nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
We had-- about a month after we were there, one day they called everybody to come to attention at the appellplatz.
And it was raining.
It was hot.
It was a hot day  then it'd rain, then there were sun showers that came out.
We were standing there drenched and drying off.
It was very hot.
And they started calling names.
And my sister said that they're already at the D's, they're not gonna call us.
A small group of this privileged group was allowed to go to Switzerland.
After a rather short stay, about a month maybe.
And they were allowed to go.
No.
I really don't.
A small number.
A small number.
A couple of hundred, a small number were allowed to go.
Again, I guess it's a token that the-- the negotiations must have broken off.
So then we stayed there until December.
Didn't know anything.
I mean, you know, they said, they're going.
The Germans were not forthcoming of what was happening to people.
Two things happened.
There was a guard whose kid had had tuberculosis.
And my father saved his life when he was a pediatrician-- a pediatric resident in Kiel, Germany, during his residency.
The guard recognized my father.
He gave him an apple.
So my sister and I shared a half an apple each.
Second unusual thing that happened to me was that my dad was allowed-- the Dutch did not have a physician.
And there was a very sick kid next door.
So I was allowed to go out from our barrack, our compound, to-- into the Dutch compound with my father.
We didn't know or didn't believe that there were gas chambers in Bergen-Belsen, that they were not gassing people.
And we were sure they were not gassing people when we were there.
And the reason for it is because being as young as I was, I could go and take showers either with my mother or my father.
And each time when my father went, I took showers.
And when the women went, I'd go along with my mother to take showers.
So, you know, I had-- had the slightest inkling that-- that not water was going to come out of those shower heads, I'm sure I would not [? do it ?].
You'd get assembled together, and then you'd march maybe five, six miles away.
And there you would undress-- just like they did in other places where they gassed the people, except here there was no gas.
So you're saying that if you had heard that, you would have been afraid.
Had my-- not, not I.
No.
Had my parents and anybody heard that this was going on, they surely would not have exposed their child to go through it twice a month.
I mean, just on a-- that today they're going to gas people.
So nobody had an inkling or thought that they were gassing people.
No.
See, we-- we were special.
The others had their uniforms.
We had no uniforms.
We wore--
All different compounds.
All different, had just like-- like-- we were very, very special, because we were not even supposed to have been there, and-- but we were there.
So that was it, the deprivation of food, not having anything to do.

Now interestingly enough, and I think this never happened to anybody during the entire war is that they allowed us to empty out the hospital barrack, and we had, in fact, Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur services.
Which, I don't think ever happened before or after in any of the other concentration camps any group of people.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] One day in November-- from time to time there would be Red Cross parcels of cigarettes, I think, that they were distributing.
And with cigarettes, you could trade.
In those days everybody was smoking.
With cigarettes it was like today you have Swiss francs or dollars.
You could trade anything for cigarettes.
And I have to tell you about my mother.
My mother was a very beautiful lady.
And no matter what happened throughout, she had firstly, we were terrors, my sister and I.
And she would say not my children.
My children are the  most wonderful children.
And the second thing, she had a tiny little piece of broken mirror and the lipstick.
And she just had to put her lipstick on and look as if she were-- wherever she was going.
Everybody, they had, obviously, we were not the first people in that compound.
So people would initial their names and dates and write names into the compounds.
One day, my mother in November gets a dream that something wonderful is going to happen.
Sure enough, 10 days later, they start giving us food.
Much greater rations and much different and much better rations than we'd ever had.
This was at the end of November of 1944.
December 4th, I believe it was-- and the dates may be off-- but it was early December, they said-- they called everybody-- told us you're leaving.
We're closing the camp and you're leaving.
Not the entire Bergen-Belsen--
One of the SS officers.
You know, they called an appel and that's it.
Everybody got their stuff together, and we marched and marched and marched with whatever we could.
And we arrived at the rail head.
And there was a terrible, terrible, terrible bombardment that night.
Terrible bombing all night.
Next morning, we got into railway cars, not cattle cars, but railway cars.
And we went to St.
Gallen, Switzerland.
We arrived and the Swiss uniforms and helmets are very similar, especially to a seven-year-old kid.
And instead of dogs, there were smiles and chocolate.
Giving kids chocolate.
I mean, and I'd say, why would the Germans want to give me chocolate?
And of course, they're Swiss, they speak Swiss German.
So, you know, to a-- an untrained ear, you know, it sounds exactly the same thing.
I go what's with these German soldiers?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
But I have to recount another story that happened.
On September 20th, 1944, I was in Bergen-Belsen, so we used to get some marmalade and maybe some margarine once in a blue moon and very black bread.
I don't know, a couple years ago, it was the hot thing, this Russian peace bread with black, horrible stuff.
So my mother and father saved up.
And it took seven slices of black bread, small portions of it, and they put a little bit of marmalade and a little bit of margarine.
And they made me a little slice of cake.
So that was my birthday present my seventh birthday.
And then we arrived at a place called St.
Gallen, which was in Switzerland.
And we stayed there several days.
And then we went to try to get people back.
We were grossly malnourished, and then they were trying to get them back into some sort of shape.
We were-- we were about a thousand.
We were about a thousand. 900 something, close to a thousand that came.
And we then were sent to a place called Caux, C-A-U-X.
Caux Montreux above the famous Montreux.
And there were three hotels there.
One was the Esplanade, which was known, also known Palais des Alpes which is now the headquarters for Moral Re-Armament.
Second one was called the Regina.
And a third one, I really don't know the name of it, the small one-- smaller one.
The overwhelming majority of the people went to the Esplanade.
A smaller number went to the Regina because this was where all the people, the Orthodox people, went there.
They were going to have kosher food there.
This was in Switzerland now.
Oh, absolutely!
No, no, no, no, no, no, no, no, they were-- these people, these were going to get kosher food.
Right.
Well, you know, I mean, we were now in Switzerland.
And the third little hotel became the hospital.
And so we stayed at the kosher hotel because my father was appointed to go there.
He was told to go there because they needed a physician.
And that's where I met Rabbi, the Satmar rabbi, [INAUDIBLE] Teitelbaum.
He'd been in Bergen-Belsen, of course, nobody could get close to him.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They tried to indoctrinate everybody.
Of course, you're to wearing tzitzit and letting your payots grow, and studying Talmud.
No, no, no, no, no, no.
I mean how could they?
How could they?
My-- my-- there was a large group on my mother's side who are very, very Hasidic.
My grandfather, as I said, was a [?
Shamus. ?] We were observant.
It's just-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]
It's just that-- absolutely, there was no conflict, no animosity.
It's just that my father always believed in freedom of thought, and freedom of will, and freedom of speech.
He was a very outspoken man.
And, you know, he was going to decide what the right thing was for his children, not some rabbi.
And the war was still on.
And of course, you couldn't come and go, because you could come and go on the grounds of the hotel, but you couldn't leave because this was-- you-- you were under martial law under Swiss military.
And they wouldn't let you leave.
[INAUDIBLE] you know-- you could not come and go.
Again, fortune shined upon us.
They needed a German-speaking physician and my father had studied in Berlin, did his residency in Kiel.
And he was fluent in German.
So he became the chief pediatrician of the hospital.
Now, the hospital was not under military control, so my sister and I were-- we were the only-- only people who were free to go as we wished.
I mean, we were not going to run away and go any place.
And we did make friends, some Swiss friends, and-- whom we are still in contact with today, who are here in New York.
And-- Just very, very nice.
I went, and I learned how to ski.
And I skied.
And we had to put our lights out.
There was a-- there was a black out in Switzerland.
Even though we were in Switzerland, there was a blackout.
And then all of a sudden, it was spring of 1945, and the war was over.
